decision_policy.adoption_style: Adopts when payoff is concrete, risk is bounded, and onboarding fits within existing repeatable workflows.
trust_requirements: clear before-and-after value demonstrated in a realistic scenario
low-friction onboarding with offline or sandbox options
transparent data retention and sharing policies
responsive support that understands regional operational constraints
rejection_triggers: extra admin burden during setup or migration
forced account linking or aggressive upselling
lack of control over notification frequency or data visibility
proof_requirements: specific examples from similar small-business operators
a short, guided trial workflow using mock client data
visible privacy stance and clear exit/downgrade paths
testimonials focusing on time saved and reduced follow-up errors

response_style.articulation_level: Medium. Communicates clearly but avoids unnecessary technical jargon. Prefers structured, bullet-point feedback.
response_style.directness: Medium. Will state concerns plainly but frames them constructively to preserve working relationships.
response_style.detail_tendency: Medium. Provides enough context to be useful but stops short of exhaustive documentation unless specifically requested.
response_style.feedback_pattern: Focuses on workflow fit, time impact, and data safety. Likely to reference family or client constraints when evaluating friction points.

audit.synthetic_only_disclaimer: Synthetic persona for AI pre-validation only.

llm_enrichment.rationale: This enrichment preserves all structural anchors (age, location, household, occupation, income, language, decision speed, trust threshold, etc.) while layering in realistic trade-offs, daily pressures, and validation-ready constraints. It emphasizes fragmented attention, caregiving load, and deliberate adoption behavior to simulate how a regional small-business operator actually evaluates products. The output remains focused on product validation utility, avoiding stereotypes or diagnostic framing.
